headline: The repricing lands: oil breaks $80 and the rupee cracks 96 as an Indian sailor is killed in Hormuz, India's Nifty slips and wholesale inflation leaps to 9.87% — with tonight's US CPI the next domino.
market_story: The 'catch-up' we flagged this morning arrived, and it was ugly around the edges. Overnight the US struck Iran a third night and Iran fired cruise missiles at two UAE tankers in the Strait of Hormuz — the sea gate for a fifth of the world's oil — killing an Indian crew member; US crude (WTI) broke above $80 for the first time in a month (now ~$80.2, +2.6%). India, shut when the news hit, opened Tuesday to the darker tape: the Nifty slipped ~0.6% to ~24,076 and the Sensex ~0.5% to ~77,257 (Bank Nifty fared worse, −1.2%), while the rupee cracked the psychological 96 mark to a record ~96.1 (each dollar now costs more rupees, so India's oil-heavy import bill balloons). The home-grown pressure deepened too: India's wholesale inflation (WPI — the bulk prices businesses pay each other, which lead shop prices) leapt to a scorching 9.87% in June, on top of retail inflation already above the RBI's 4% target — a combination that boxes in the central bank just as growth needs help. Cross-asset, the tells are textbook risk-off with a twist: Wall Street's VIX 'fear gauge' sits elevated near 17 (+14%), US chips led Monday's drop (Nasdaq −1.6%), and gold — which fell on Monday as a hawkish Fed lifted rate-hike bets — is steadying (~$4,030, +0.8%) as it recovers from a two-week low ahead of tonight's big event: the US CPI inflation report. That print is the next domino: a hot number would cement 'higher for longer' US rates, lifting the dollar and pressuring the rupee and stocks further; a soft one would relieve pressure. For the user's book: Nvidia slipped again to ~$204 (−3.5%), back below its 50-day average, with a fresh company-specific worry — an FT report it has halved its approved Asia AI-chip customer list; Bitcoin steadied at ~$62.6k (+0.6%) but stayed in its downtrend, never acting as the war hedge; Apple was again the quiet winner, up ~0.6% to a fresh 52-week high as money hid in steady mega-caps; and Reliance, finally trading, held roughly flat (~₹1,301, +0.3%) as metals and energy names cushioned the fall. The lesson underneath: a war reaches markets in stages — first the oil price, then the currency, then the inflation data — and tonight's US CPI is where the next stage gets priced.

board.name: S&P 500
board.read: US large-caps fell on Monday's war-driven risk-off; the index still sits above its long-term averages (a healthy uptrend), so this is a pullback, not a breakdown.

board.name: Nasdaq
board.read: The tech-heavy index fell twice as hard as the S&P as chipmakers led the drop — high-flying AI names fall fastest when fear hits.

board.name: Dow Jones
board.read: The old-economy Dow held up best — steadier, less tech-heavy businesses are where money hides on a risk-off day.

board.name: Nifty 50
board.read: India opened lower to 'catch up' to the overnight oil shock, but the fall was orderly — domestic buying and metal stocks cushioned it.

board.name: Sensex
board.read: The 30-share benchmark slipped as oil-sensitive names fell; a controlled decline rather than a crash.

board.name: Bank Nifty
board.read: Banks fell hardest — sticky inflation and higher-for-longer rates squeeze lenders whose customers borrow to buy.

board.name: USD/INR
board.read: The rupee cracked the 96 mark to a record low — costlier oil means India must sell more rupees to buy dollars, and a firm dollar adds pressure.

board.name: Crude Oil (WTI)
board.read: Broke above $80 for the first time in a month after Iran hit two tankers in Hormuz — the war premium is finally being paid.

board.name: Gold
board.read: Steadying off a two-week low ahead of tonight's US inflation data; it fell Monday because a hawkish Fed lifts rates, which makes no-yield gold less attractive.

board.name: Bitcoin
board.read: A small bounce but still stuck in a downtrend near its 52-week low — notably, it did NOT rally as a war hedge, behaving like a risk asset.

board.name: Ethereum
board.read: Edged up with Bitcoin but remains in a sideways, subdued range — crypto risk appetite stays cautious.

board.name: VIX (volatility)
board.read: Wall Street's 'fear gauge' jumped ~14% — still historically moderate (~17), so this is heightened caution, not panic.

themes.note: Oil above $80 lifts oil & gas producers and defence names, and metal stocks rose up to 3% in India as global commodity prices climbed — the relative winners on a war day.
Chips led the Nasdaq lower (Nvidia −3.5%) as war risk-off met an FT report that Nvidia halved its Asia AI-chip customer list — high-beta names fall first and hardest.
Hot Indian wholesale inflation (9.87%) plus 'higher-for-longer' US rate fears pressure lenders and borrowers; Bank Nifty fell over 1%.
Money hid in steady names — Apple made a fresh 52-week high even as chips fell, a textbook risk-off rotation into quality.

what_to_watch.title: Oil breaks $80 as the war hits tankers
key_points: WTI crude broke above $80 (~$80.2, +2.6%) — a one-month high
Iran fired cruise missiles at two UAE tankers in Hormuz; an Indian sailor was killed
Hormuz shipping transits fell to a one-month low
Energy & defence names gain; airlines, paints, tyres, logistics get squeezed
what_to_watch.gist: US crude (WTI) broke decisively above $80 a barrel — its highest in a month — after Iran attacked two oil tankers in the Strait of Hormuz, the channel that carries about a fifth of the world's oil. Unlike last week's 'closed strait' threats that markets shrugged off, a physical hit on shipping (and an Indian crew death) is a concrete supply risk, so the war premium in oil finally got paid.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: Costlier oil flows straight into every oil-importing economy — India buys ~85% of its crude abroad — via a bigger fuel bill, a weaker currency and higher inflation. It also revives the fear that central banks keep rates higher for longer to fight fuel-driven inflation, the channel now pressuring stocks and gold.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: For an Indian reader, oil above $80 is the single most important number today: it drives the rupee, inflation and the cost of everything imported. Watch whether it holds above $80 or fades.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether WTI holds above $80, if Iran hits more ships, and any Oman/Qatar move to de-escalate the Hormuz toll dispute.

key_terms.definition: A narrow sea lane between Iran and Oman carrying about a fifth of the world's seaborne oil; there is no easy way around it.
The extra amount buyers pay for oil to insure against conflict cutting supply; it jumps when a feared cutoff turns from possible to concrete.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: Markets react to the marginal surprise, not the repeated headline — a threat in words moves prices little, but a concrete act (a tanker hit) that raises real supply risk forces the repricing all at once.

sources.name: The Hindu (Oil hits one-month high as US, Iran step up attacks in Hormuz)
Business Standard (1 Indian killed, 6 injured after Iranian missiles hit UAE tankers in Hormuz)

what_to_watch.title: The rupee cracks 96 to a record low
key_points: Rupee fell ~48 paise past 96 to a record ~96.1 per dollar
Closed Monday at 95.68; oil above $80 is the driver
A weaker rupee raises the cost of all imports and feeds inflation
Dollar-earning IT and pharma exporters are the relative winners
what_to_watch.gist: The rupee broke through the psychological 96-per-dollar level for the first time, falling about 48 paise to ~96.1 in early trade. When each dollar costs more rupees, India's oil-heavy import bill grows and imported goods get pricier — the currency is the most sensitive gauge of the oil shock.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: A record-low rupee widens the trade deficit (India's June import bill surged ~27% to $88.8bn on oil and gold) and adds to inflation, narrowing the RBI's room to cut rates. It pressures importers, airlines and paint/tyre makers, while flattering dollar-earning IT and pharma exporters.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: A weak rupee quietly taxes every Indian consumer through costlier fuel and imports — but it is a tailwind for the IT stocks that cushion the index. Watch whether it steadies below 96.5.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether the rupee holds below ~96.5, RBI intervention, and the crude level — the two are tightly linked.

key_terms.definition: When each dollar buys more rupees (here past 96), imports get costlier and inflation rises, though exporters earning in dollars gain.
The gap when a country imports more than it exports; a wider gap means more dollars flowing out, which pressures the currency.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: In an oil-importing economy the currency is the shock absorber — a war you can't see reaches your wallet first through a falling rupee, before it ever shows up in an official inflation number.

sources.name: The Hindu (Rupee falls 48 paise to 96.16 against US dollar in early trade)

what_to_watch.title: US inflation (CPI) due tonight — the next domino
key_points: US CPI report due tonight; the market's next big catalyst
A Fed official signalled rates may need to go higher on the oil spike
US June jobs were weak (57,000 added, 4.2% unemployment)
Gold steadied off a two-week low; UK & global bond yields rose
what_to_watch.gist: Tonight's US Consumer Price Index (CPI) — the main gauge of American inflation — will shape whether the Federal Reserve leans toward hiking rates. The setup is tense: a war-driven oil spike argues for higher rates to fight inflation, but June US jobs were weak (just 57,000 added), which argues for lower — the classic stagflation squeeze.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: A hot CPI would cement 'higher for longer' rates, lifting bond yields (the return investors demand to lend) and the dollar, and pressuring stocks and emerging-market currencies like the rupee. A soft print would relieve that pressure. This is why gold, which fell Monday on rate-hike fears, is steadying ahead of the number.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: Tonight's US number will set the tone for India's Wednesday open — a hot print means more rupee and stock pressure; a soft one, relief. It's the single biggest swing factor into midweek.
what_to_watch.watch: The CPI print versus expectations, the reaction in US 10-year bond yields and the dollar, and any Fed commentary on hikes.

key_terms.definition: The main measure of how fast the prices households pay are rising; a hot US CPI raises the odds the Fed keeps rates high.
The painful mix of high inflation and weak growth at once — it traps central banks because fighting one problem worsens the other.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: Markets price the FUTURE — a single data release can move the world before any actual policy change, which is why a calmer or hotter inflation print can swing stocks days before the Fed acts.

sources.name: Reuters (Gold recovers from two-week low ahead of US inflation figures)

what_to_watch.title: Nvidia slips again as the AI trade wobbles
key_points: Nvidia fell ~3.5% to ~$204, back below its 50-day average (~$209)
FT: Nvidia has halved its approved Asia AI-chip customer list
Chips led the Nasdaq's drop; SanDisk also plunged
Apple, a defensive mega-cap, made a fresh 52-week high instead
what_to_watch.gist: Nvidia slid about 3.5% to ~$204, falling back below its 50-day average (a medium-term trend line at ~$209), pressured by both the war risk-off and an FT report that it has halved its list of approved AI-chip customers in Asia — a company-specific worry (tighter export rules or a narrower sales base) on top of the macro one.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: AI stocks are 'high-beta' — they swing more than the market both ways — so they fall fastest on fear days. A shrinking customer list raises questions about Nvidia's future sales, and because Nvidia leads the whole AI complex, its wobble drags the Nasdaq. The AI build-out is still real, but the stocks that ran furthest are the most exposed when sentiment turns.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: For a holder, Nvidia is now range-bound and sentiment-driven — a reclaim of ~$209 would signal the dip is buyable; failure to hold ~$201 support risks a deeper slide. Rotation out of pricey AI names can favour cheaper markets like India.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether Nvidia reclaims its ~$209 50-day average or loses ~$201 support, and details on the Asia customer cuts.

key_terms.definition: A stock that moves more than the market — it rises faster in rallies and falls faster in sell-offs; most AI names have high beta.
The average closing price over the last 50 days; trading below it signals the medium-term trend has weakened.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: When fear hits, not all stocks fall equally — the highest-flying, most speculative names drop first and hardest, while steadier businesses (like Apple) hold up or even rise.

sources.name: Reuters (Nvidia halves Asia AI chip customer list, FT reports)

what_to_watch.title: India's wholesale inflation leaps to 9.87%
key_points: Wholesale inflation (WPI) surged to 9.87% in June on high food prices
Wholesale fuel-and-power inflation ran at 27.41%
Retail (CPI) inflation is already above the RBI's 4% target at 4.38%
The combination narrows the RBI's room to cut rates
what_to_watch.gist: India's wholesale price inflation (WPI — the bulk, factory-gate prices businesses charge each other) jumped to 9.87% in June, driven by food and still-hot fuel-and-power costs. Wholesale prices lead the retail prices households pay, so this is an early-warning siren that shop-level inflation — already above the RBI's 4% target — may stay high.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: Two inflation engines are firing at once: food (a weak monsoon) and fuel (the Hormuz oil spike). That combination ties the Reserve Bank of India's hands — instead of cutting rates to support growth, it may have to hold or hike, raising borrowing costs for homebuyers, businesses and the government.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: Sticky inflation is a headwind for rate-sensitive banks, autos and real estate and for the rupee — and a reason the RBI can't ride to the market's rescue with rate cuts anytime soon.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether wholesale pressure feeds into July's retail CPI, the monsoon's recovery, and the RBI's tone at its next meeting.

key_terms.definition: Tracks bulk, factory-gate prices businesses charge each other; it moves before shop prices and is heavily swayed by fuel and food.
A central bank keeping rates elevated to fight inflation, which raises borrowing costs across the economy.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: Inflation has a pipeline: wholesale prices lead, retail prices follow. A hot WPI warns that shop prices may stay high — and when fuel and food inflation fire together, a central bank can be boxed in even when growth needs lower rates.

sources.name: Business Standard (India's wholesale inflation rises to 9.87% in June on high food prices)

global_summary.us: US stocks fell Monday on the war risk-off — the S&P 500 −0.8% and the Nasdaq −1.6% as chips led the drop and the VIX 'fear gauge' jumped ~14% to ~17. All eyes now turn to tonight's US CPI inflation report, with a weak June jobs print (57,000 added) muddying the Fed's path.
global_summary.india: The Nifty (~24,076, −0.6%) and Sensex (~77,257, −0.5%) opened lower to 'catch up' to oil above $80, with Bank Nifty (−1.2%) hit hardest and metal stocks the cushion. The rupee cracked 96 to a record low, and wholesale inflation leapt to 9.87% — a heavy macro backdrop.
global_summary.crypto: Bitcoin steadied at ~$62.6k (+0.6%) and Ether at ~$1,782, but both stayed subdued and range-bound — crypto behaved like a risk asset, not a war hedge.
global_summary.macro: Oil (WTI) broke above $80 on the Hormuz tanker strikes; the dollar held firm and gold steadied off a two-week low ahead of US CPI; UK and Indian bond yields rose as 'higher for longer' rate fears spread.

catalysts_today.event: Strait of Hormuz — tanker attacks, blockade enforcement, oil above $80
India Q1 earnings continue (Infosys, banks after HCLTech's beat)

favorites.name: Apple Inc.
favorites.whats_new: The quiet winner of the risk-off: Apple rose ~0.6% to a fresh 52-week high (~$317) as money hid in steady mega-caps while chips and high-beta tech fell. It extended this morning's small gain even as the Nasdaq dropped.

news.headline: Apple sues OpenAI, alleging an ex-engineer stole trade secrets tied to OpenAI's hardware push

favorites.fundamentals: A ~$4.66T mega-cap on a rich P/E of ~38 (forward ~33), revenue growing ~17% a year with fat ~27% profit margins; a premium price for a quality, defensive business. Tiny 0.3% dividend.
favorites.technicals: Strong Buy, confirmed uptrend: price sits above both its 50-day (~$299) and 200-day (~$273) averages at the very top of its 52-week range (100th percentile). RSI ~64 is neutral (not yet overbought), but shorter-term oscillators (Stochastic, CCI, Williams %R) are flashing overbought — a caution that a pause or pullback could come. First support ~$314, resistance ~$322.
favorites.catalysts: Next earnings; the OpenAI lawsuit and its AI-hardware ambitions; iOS 27 / Siri AI rollout.
favorites.risks: Stretched at a 52-week high with overbought short-term signals; a broad risk-off deepening or a hot CPI could trigger profit-taking. A close below the ~$299 50-day average would warn the uptrend is stalling.

favorites.name: NVIDIA Corp.
favorites.whats_new: Slid another ~3.5% to ~$204, back below its 50-day average, on the war risk-off plus an FT report that it has halved its approved Asia AI-chip customer list — a company-specific worry stacked on the macro one. It never reclaimed Friday's breakout.

news.headline: Nvidia halves Asia AI chip customer list, FT reports

favorites.fundamentals: A ~$4.93T mega-cap growing revenue an eye-watering ~85% a year with ~63% profit margins; the trailing P/E of ~31 drops to a forward ~16, meaning the market expects profits to keep surging. High beta (~2.2) — it swings far more than the market.
favorites.technicals: Buy but transitional: price is caught between its 50-day (~$209) and 200-day (~$192) averages — no clear trend, so it can break either way. RSI ~50 is neutral and trend strength is weak. First support ~$201, resistance ~$208; a daily close back above ~$209 would be the first sign the dip is turning up.
favorites.catalysts: Clarity on the Asia customer-list report and export rules; upcoming AI-chip earnings; tonight's CPI (rate moves hit high-growth valuations).
favorites.risks: Highest-beta name in the book on a fear day; the customer-list report adds a demand question, and a deeper risk-off could push it below ~$201 support toward the 200-day average.

favorites.name: Bitcoin
favorites.whats_new: Steadied with a small ~0.6% bounce to ~$62.6k after Monday's slide, but stayed firmly in its downtrend and near its 52-week low — the key tell is that it did NOT rally as a safe haven during the war, trading like a risk asset instead.

news.headline: Bitcoin holds near $62.5k as war risk-off keeps crypto subdued; behaves as a risk asset, not a hedge

favorites.fundamentals: About a $1.26T asset; no earnings or cash flows — its price is driven by risk appetite, liquidity and flows rather than fundamentals.
favorites.technicals: Sell, textbook downtrend: price is below both its 50-day (~$64,288) and 200-day (~$73,617) averages, sitting at just the 8th percentile of its 52-week range (near the low). RSI ~47 is neutral. It's clinging to ~$62.3k support with resistance at ~$62.8k; a close back above the ~$64,288 50-day average is what it would take to signal a turn.
favorites.catalysts: US CPI and the dollar's direction (a firmer dollar and higher yields weigh on crypto); overall risk appetite.
favorites.risks: Sitting near a 52-week low in a confirmed downtrend; a deeper risk-off or a hot CPI could push it toward fresh lows. Its failure to act as a war hedge undercuts the 'digital gold' thesis for now.

favorites.name: Reliance Industries
favorites.whats_new: Finally trading after Monday's shut session, Reliance held roughly flat (~₹1,301, +0.3%) despite the oil shock — as an energy-and-refining major, higher crude is a mixed blessing, and metal/energy resilience cushioned the broader fall.

news.headline: India's HCLTech beats and enters data-centre business; Reliance-backed Jio IPO complex in focus

favorites.fundamentals: A ~₹17.6T energy major on a moderate P/E of ~22 (forward ~18), revenue growing ~12% with thin ~8% margins (typical of refining). Low beta (~0.18) — much steadier than the market.
favorites.technicals: Sell, downtrend: price is below both its 50-day (~₹1,325) and 200-day (~₹1,415) averages, at the 13th percentile of its 52-week range (near the low). RSI ~48 is neutral and MACD momentum has ticked up. First support ~₹1,291, resistance ~₹1,309; a close above the ~₹1,325 50-day average would be the first sign of a turn.
favorites.catalysts: Crude's direction (mixed for refining margins), the awaited Jio Platforms IPO, and the broader energy-transition push.
favorites.risks: Stuck near a 52-week low in a downtrend; a sustained oil spike squeezes refining margins even as it lifts the energy narrative, and a weak rupee/market keeps sentiment soft.

risks: Oil sustaining above $80 (or a genuine Hormuz supply cut) would deepen the rupee's slide, widen India's import bill and reignite inflation across the book.
A hot US CPI tonight would cement 'higher for longer' rates — lifting the dollar and yields and pressuring stocks, gold, crypto and the rupee.
Sticky Indian inflation (WPI 9.87%, CPI above target) boxes in the RBI, a headwind for rate-sensitive banks, autos and real estate.
AI/chip leadership wobbling (Nvidia's halved Asia list) could drag the Nasdaq and spill into global risk sentiment.
Further escalation in the Strait of Hormuz — more tanker strikes or a blockade clash — remains the single biggest cross-asset tail risk.
disclaimer: For information only. Not financial advice. Data may be delayed and can be inaccurate; verify before acting.
